subject: Gastroenterology
title: Gallbladder and bile
preambleMarkdown:
# Gallbladder and bile



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Gallbladder and bile is a clinical condition or syndrome in gastroenterology.
- This article focuses on GI anatomy, motility, inflammation, bleeding, obstruction, liver-pancreas-biliary disease, and nutrition.
- The practical clinical priority is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Gallbladder and bile to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Gallbladder and bile is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Gallbladder and bile depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In gastroenterology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Gallbladder and bile.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Gallbladder and bile disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Gallbladder and bile should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Gallbladder and bile first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Gallbladder and bile, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Gallbladder and bile, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused gastroenterology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Gallbladder and bile presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Gallbladder and bile with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Gallbladder and bile is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Gallbladder and bile should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Gallbladder and bile is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses gastroenterology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Gallbladder and bile, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Gallbladder and bile may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Gallbladder and bile patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Gallbladder and bile, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Gallbladder and bile in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Gallbladder and bile, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Gallbladder and bile does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Gallbladder and bile may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Gallbladder and bile, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Gallbladder and bile as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Gallbladder and bile draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References

- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Biliary System

_Source lines: 10440-10862._

```text
BILIARY SYSTEM
I. General Principles
A. Anatomy

## 1 . Hepatic ducts

a. The left hepatic duct is formed from the union of the three segmental ducts
draining segments 2, 3 , and 4.

b . The right hepatic duct is formed from the union of segmental ducts
draining 5, 6, 7, and 8 .

c. Segment 1 , the caudate lobe, drains most commonly into both the right and
left hepatic ducts.

d. Both the right and left hepatic ducts j oin to form the common hepatic duct.

## 2. Gallbladder

a. The gallbladder is a pear-shaped, distensible reservoir structure with an
average capacity of 30 to 50 mL.

b . The inferior and lateral surface is covered by peritoneum, and the superior
surface is associated with the hepatic fossa.

c. This organ has four anatomic portions : the fundus , body, infundibulum,
and neck.

d. It is supplied by the cystic artery, which is most commonly a branch of the RHA.

3. Cystic duct
a. This arises from the gallbladder and drains into the common hepatic duct
to form the common bile duct ( CBD) .

b. Its average length is 2 to 4 em.

## 4. CBD

a. The CBD is formed by the union of the cystic duct and the common hepatic
duct.

b . It is approximately 8 em in length, with an average diameter of 4 to 9 mm
in adults and 3 to 5 mm in children. After cholecystectomy, it may dilate an
additional 2 to 3 mm in size.

c. It most commonly j oins the pancreatic duct to open into the second portion
of the duodenum at the ampulla of Vater. The opening is regulated by the
sphincter of Oddi.

## 5 . Triangle of Calot: This region is bordered by the common hepatic duct medi

ally, the cystic duct laterally, and the liver edge superiorly.

## 6. Sphincter of Oddi

a. Located at the ampulla of Vater
b. Length of 4 to 6 mm
c. The basal resting pressure is 13 mm Hg.

d. Regulates the flow of bile and pancreatic juice
e. Relaxation occurs with cholecystokinin and vagal stimulation. Sympathetic
stimulation causes increased pressure and sphincter constriction.

## 7 . Anatomic variants of biliary anatomy (Fig. 8-7)

B. Physiology
l . The gallbladder absorbs water and solute from bile, thereby concentrating the
solute components.

2. It acts as a storage organ for bile.

3. In addition, it secretes mucus and glycoproteins .

4. Gallbladder contractility is controlled by enteric hormones and autonomic
nervous system. Primarily, these stimulants are cholecystokinin and vagal Hring.

II. Gallstone Disease
A. Types
Gallstones are classified according to their cholesterol content as either pigment
or cholesterol stones . Most of the gallstones are mixed, containing calcium salts
in their center.

l . Pure cholesterol stones constitute only 1 0% of all gallstones. However, 70% to
80% of the gallstones are considered to be cholesterol stones.

A d i l ation of the C B D g reater

(With permission from M u l h o l l a n d MW, Lillemoe KD, D o h e rty GM, M a i e r RV, U p c h u rc h G R. eds. Greenfield's Surgery: Scientific
Principles and Practice. 4th e d . P h i l a d e l p h i a , PA: Li p p i n c ott Williams & Wilkins; 2006. )
2 . Approximately 20% to 30% gallstones are pigment stones. There are two types
of pigment gallstones:
a. Black stones consist of calcium bilirubinate and calcium palmitate . They
are frequently small and multiple and tend to occur almost exclusively in
the gallbladder. Risk factors for black stones are hemolytic anemia and
cirrhosis.

b . Brown stones are more common in the Asian population, in the setting of
biliary dysmotility and chronic bacterial and parasitic infections. These may
also occur as primary common bile duct stones .

B. Pathogenesis : A combination of the following factors favor the development of
the stones :
l . Cholesterol supersaturation in the bile
2. Crystal nucleation due to aggregation and precipitation of the cholesterol-rich
vesicles
3. Gallbladder dysmotility
4. Gallbladder absorptive dysfunction
5. Percentages of saturation of three elements in bile lead to precipitation and
cholesterol stone formation. These three elements are lecithin, cholesterol, and
bile salt. Normally, a delicate balance exists between the levels of bile acids,
phospholipids, and cholesterol (see Admirand triangle) . When this balance is
disrupted, especially when there is supersaturation with cholesterol, there is
predisposition for the formation of lithogenic bile and the consequent develop-

H E P A T O B I L I A R Y S Y S T E M e 1 43
ment of cholesterol-type gallstones . This is because when cholesterol supersat
urates, it tends to crystallize, and in the presence of enucleating factors, it can
be a nidus for stone formation. The small, purple-shaded area is the only area
where cholesterol remains entirely soluble.

C. Clinical presentation
l . Asymptomatic
a. The vast majority of patients with gallstones are asymptomatic.

b . Twenty percent of these patients become symptomatic in 20 years.

c. Of this group of patients , l % to 2% develop serious symptoms or complica
tions related to their gallstones.

## 2. Symptomatic

a. Biliary colic
( l ) This condition is due to intermittent obstruction of the cystic duct with
passage of small stones.

(2) There is intermittent, spasmodic RUQ pain that occurs most commonly
after a fatty meal and may last for a few hours. It is associated with
nausea, vomiting, and bloating.

b. Acute cholecystitis
( l ) This condition is due to occlusion of the cystic duct, and this incites an
inflammatory response. Bile may become infected with gram-negative
bacteria , most commonly E. coli.

(2) Patients present with RUQ pain, which is usually longer lasting, and
they may have constitutional signs of fever and tachycardia . Abdominal
examination usually reveals RUQ tenderness.

(3) Laboratory findings include an elevated white blood cell count and
mild hyperbilirubinemia.

D . Diagnosis
l . RUQ ultrasound is the most useful diagnostic test. Ultrasound has 85% speci
ficity and 95% sensitivity in diagnosing acute cholecystitis. Findings suggestive
of acute cholecystitis are :
a. Presence of gallstones
b. Thickening of the gallbladder wall greater than 4 mm
c. Pericholecystic fluid
2. Radionucleotide scanning or a HIDA scan may also be helpful.

a. The radionucleotide is administered intravenously, and the patient is
scanned in 30 minutes.

b . Nonfilling of the gallbladder with the radiotracer but filling of the CBD and
the duodenum indicates an obstructed cystic duct and acute cholecystitis .

c. Highly sensitive and specific, about 95% for each, for acute cholecystitis
E. Management of symptomatic gallstones
Medical management of severe acute cholecystitis includes hospital admission, no
oral intake , intravenous (IV) fluids , and use of broad-spectrum antibiotics.

l . Surgical management
a. Early cholecystectomy, within 3 days of presentation, is advocated by some
authors and may be accomplished before onset of second phase of acute
inflammatory response. Initial phase often includes pericholecystic fluid or
tissue edema, which makes surgical removal easier.

b. Relative indications for prophylactic cholecystectomy are:
( l ) Pediatric gallstone disease
(2) Congenital hemolytic anemias (e.g. , sickle-cell disease)
(3) Gallstones greater than 2 . 5 em, which increases the incidence of cancer
of gallbladder and acute cholecystitis
(4) Porcelain gallbladder, which increases the incidence of gallbladder
cancer
(5) Bariatric surgery
(6) Note: Asymptomatic gallstone disease in diabetics is no longer
considered an indication for prophylactic cholecystectomy.

M u r p h y s i g n , or i n s p i ratory
a r rest d u ri n g deep p a l p a
t i o n of the R U Q, is t h e
c l a s s i c a l f i n d i n g i n a c ute
c h o l e c ystiti s .

c. If patients present with symptoms of more than 3 days duration, it may be
prudent to treat the gallbladder disease conservatively (medical management
as described earlier) and perform cholecystectomy electively in 4 to 6 weeks.

d. In certain high-risk patients , or in severely ill patients in the intensive care
unit whose conditions do not permit cholecystectomy, percutaneous cho
lecystostomy may be performed to relieve obstruction and decompress the
gallbladder.

e. Cholecystectomy could be performed either with the laparoscope or in an
open fashion. Laparoscopic cholecystectomy has the advantage of short hos
pital stay and less morbidity associated due to smaller wounds.

F. Complications
a. Empyema of the gallbladder
b. Emphysematous cholecystitis: This is more commonly seen in patients with
diabetes mellitus. Patients present with RUQ pain and sepsis. Abdominal
Hlms or CT scan may show air in the gallbladder wall or lumen. Infection is
generally due to Escherichia coli, Enterococcus, Klebsiella, or Clostridia species .

c. Gangrene and perforation: The gallbladder may perforate into adjacent
viscera such as the duodenum and hepatic flexure of the colon (two most
common sites) .

d. Small bowel obstruction: Chronic erosion of the gallbladder wall by a 2-cm
or larger gallstone can also form the cholecystenteric Hstula. This is seen in
up to 2% of patients with acute cholecystitis. Following fistula formation,
the large stone may tumble through the GI tract until it reaches the ileum,
with the narrowest luminal size. Here, the stone may cause small bowel
obstruction, which is termed gallstone ileus. This occurs in up to 1 5 % of
patients with cholecystenteric fistulas. The obstruction is described as epi
sodic and recurrent because the impacted stone temporarily impacts in the
gut lumen and then dislodges and moves forward. This is known as tum
bling obstruction. Abdominal imaging may show evidence of small bowel
obstruction and presence of pneumobilia. Surgical management focuses on
relieving the acute bowel obstruction and includes proximal enterotomy
with stone removal. Takedown and repair of the biliary enteric fistula and
cholecystectomy are best deferred to a later date after the patient recovers
from the acute obstruction.

G . Acute cholangitis
l . Acute cholangitis is a bacterial infection of the biliary ductal system due to
ductal obstruction and significant bacterial concentration in the bile. The most
common causes of biliary obstruction are choledocholithiasis, benign stric
tures , and tumors.

2. Patient may present with fever, RUQ pain, and jaundice (Charcot triad) and
later with mental status changes and hypotension (Reynolds pentad) . Initial
management is resuscitative with IV antibiotics, fluids , and systemic support.

Urgent clearance of the infected and obstructed biliary tract is paramount.

Options for biliary decompression include endoscopic retrograde cholangio
pancreatography (ERCP) , percutaneous transhepatic cholangiogram (PTC) ,
or surgical decompression. Once the patient is stabilized, surgical removal of
the gallbladder (during the same hospitalization) is recommended to prevent
recurrent obstructions and episodes of cholangitis.

H . Inflammatory lesions of biliary tract: PSC
l. PSC is a chronic progressive cholestatic disease in which the inflammatory pro
cess involves the bile ducts , resulting in intrahepatic or extrahepatic strictures.

2. This autoimmune disease is associated with other inflammatory bowel dis
ease, with 85% of the patients having ulcerative colitis and 1 5% patients hav
ing Crohn disease. Males are more commonly affected than females, and PSC
tends to present in the fourth decade of life . Prevalence is 10 per 1 00,000
population.

3. Patients present with signs and symptoms of biliary obstruction, jaundice, pru
ritus, weight loss, and fatigue .

H E P A T O B I L I A R Y S Y S T E M e 1 45
4. Diagnosis is based on liver biopsy, which may show presence of cholestasis
and cirrhosis. ERCP or MRCP shows the characteristic beaded appearance of
the ductal system Xom the areas of strictures and dilatation.

5. Management: The only definitive cure is liver transplant. Medical management
with immunosuppressives and bile acids , such as ursodeoxycholic acid, has
shown some temporary beneHt.

6. Complications may be secondary to :
a. Choledocholithiasis
b. Postoperative biliary strictures
c. Infections such as ascending cholangitis and parasitic infections
d. Iatrogenic operative ischemia of the bile ducts
e. Drugs, chemotherapy with intra-arterial 5-fluorouracil
f. AIDS-associated cholangiopathy
I. Malignant biliary disease

## 1 . Gallbladder cancer

a. Incidence
( 1 ) Women are affected more than men, in part due to their higher inci
dence of gallstones. The overall incidence of cancer is 2 . 5 cases per a
population of 1 0 0 ,000.

(2) The disease is more common in Native Americans .

(3) The highest incidence occurs in residents of Chile.

b . Etiology
( 1 ) Risk factors include gallstones greater than 2 . 5 em, porcelain
gallbladder, choledochal cysts, PSC, and cholecystenteric fistula.

(2) Approximately 1% of all elective cholecystectomies performed for
cholelithiasis harbor an occult gallbladder cancer.

c. Pathology
( 1 ) Ninety percent of the cancers of the gallbladder are adenocarcinoma.

(2) At diagnosis, 25% of the cancers are localized to the gallbladder
wall, 35% have associated metastases to the regional lymph nodes or
extension into adjacent organs , and 40% have already metastasized to
distant sites.

d. Presentation
( 1 ) Most often, patients present with RUQ abdominal pain, often
mimicking other common biliary and nonbiliary disease.

(2) Other symptoms may include weight loss, jaundice, and abdominal
mass .

e. Diagnosis : Ultrasound or CT usually demonstrates a mass in the gallblad
der. In addition, a CT scan shows the extension into the surrounding struc
tures and the vascular anatomy.

f. Management
( 1 ) Patients with a preoperatively suspected cancer of the gallbladder
should undergo open cholecystectomy to minimize the chance of bile
spillage and tumor dissemination.

(2) If cancer of the gallbladder is diagnosed after a cholecystectomy, and
on pathologic examination of the specimen the lesion is confined to
the mucosa, submucosa, or the muscularis (T 1 ) , then cholecystec
tomy is adequate therapy. These patients have an 85% to 1 00% 5-year
survival rate.

(3) Cancer of the gallbladder with invasion beyond the muscularis is
associated with an increasing incidence of regional lymph node metas
tases and needs an extended cholecystectomy with a 2-cm margin of
the adjacent liver tissue with lymphadenectomy.

( 4) Palliative care
(a) In patients with an unresectable tumor, the obstructive jaun
dice can be managed with biliary decompression through either
percutaneous or endoscopically placed biliary stents .

(b) Pain should be aggressively treated.

## 2. Cholangiocarcinoma

a. This uncommon cancer is located commonly at the hepatic duct
bifurcation.

b . Incidence
( l ) Each year, 2,500 to 3 ,000 new cases are diagnosed.

(2) The carcinoma occurs with equal frequency in men and women, and
the incidence increases with age.

c. Etiology
( l ) Risk factors include PSC, choledochal cysts, hepatolithiasis , and pre
vious biliary enteric anastomosis . Other risk factors include parasitic
disease, liver flukes , dietary nitrosamines, dioxin, and thorium dioxide
(Thorotrast) .

(2) Common factors in all cases are bile stasis , infection, stones , and
chronic inflammation.

d. Classification consists of three broad groups:
( l ) Intrahepatic: 6%
(2) Perihilar: 67%
(3) Distal: 27%
e. Clinical presentation: Ninety percent of patients present with jaundice,
pruritus, fever, and abdominal pain. In addition, they may present with
acute cholangitis .

f. Diagnosis
( l ) CT is the initial diagnostic test of choice. This reveals dilated bile
ducts, lymph nodes, and vascular anatomy.

(2) A cholangiogram obtained through either the percutaneous transhe
patic route or the endoscopic retrograde route further defines the
biliary anatomy.

g. Management
( l ) For the intrahepatic group, a partial hepatectomy is indicated.

(2) For the perihilar group , a resection and hepaticoj ejunostomy is
indicated.

(3) For the distal group, a pancreaticoduodenectomy is indicated.
```
